---
title: "CTL + ALT + DELISH"
subtitle: "Lunch and Learn"
author: "Jonathan Jayes"
date: today
format:
  nexer-revealjs: default
---

# What are we aiming for?

![](assets/screenshots/Rasmus-meditating-at-desk-with-laptop.png){fig-alt="Person meditating at desk with laptop"}

## To save time going from "What shall we have for lunch?" to "Order placed."

![](assets/screenshots/Rasmus-meditating-at-desk-with-laptop-smiling.png){fig-alt="Person meditating at desk with laptop smiling"}

# What shall we try first?

## Just ask Copilot in MS Teams

![](assets/screenshots/01.png){fig-alt="Screenshot of MS Teams Copilot interface"}

## Hmm, it can't create the poll...

![](assets/screenshots/02.png){fig-alt="Screenshot of MS Teams Copilot interface with lunch suggestions"}

## But it can find the menu, right?

![](assets/screenshots/03.png){fig-alt="Screenshot of MS Teams Copilot interface with menu suggestions"}


## But it can find the menu, RIGHT?

![](assets/screenshots/04.png){fig-alt="Screenshot of MS Teams Copilot interface with menu suggestions"}

## Wrong!

The lunch menu has a smaller selection of burgers, and all go for 125 SEK.

```{r}
knitr::include_url("https://malmo.docksideburgers.se/lunchmeny/", height = "500px")

```

# So, Copilot in MS Teams can't help us with lunch decisions yet.

![](assets/screenshots/microsoft-teams-premium-copilot-1024x538.png)

# What could we do instead?

## Deterministic approach?

We could write a scraper to visit the restaurant websites, and extract the menu information from there.

We could build a FastAPI app that serves the menu information in a structured format, and manually copy into a poll in MS Teams.

## Where does this solution fail?

The website menus are quite varied, for example, 

::: {.incremental}

- Dockside's menu is a PDF that updates monthly

- Holy Greens' menu is a javascript page that shows available options and prices per restaurant location

- Reka's Burgers menu is a static HTML page that hasn't changed since last year

- **We could write custom scrapers for each restaurant, but that would be a lot of work, and would break easily if the website changes.**

:::


## So, let's try giving the system more agency to achieve specific goals

Follow along on Github at [github.com/j-jayes/literate-broccoli](https://github.com/j-jayes/literate-broccoli)

::: footer
![](assets/github_link.png)
:::

# Section on the scraper

Here we explain how we use the scraper that is flexible, searches for the restauran't lunch menu, and extracts the relevant information. Here the flexibility is provided by Gemini as an LLM, which can understand the structure of the website, and extract the relevant information, even if the website changes.

Here we also want to explain how the flexibility is a benefit, and how it allows us to easily add new restaurants to the system.

It should be clear that this scraper is deployed as an MCP server that is called on by the Copilot Studio agent, and that the agent is responsible for deciding when to call the scraper, and how to use the information it returns.

It should also be clear that this scraper is used in the solution with the frontend and backend, inside `lunch-web-app`, and that because our MCP server is written in FastMCP, we can easily integrate it with our FastAPI backend, and use the same codebase for both the scraper and the backend.

We should talk about the value of a pydantic data model for the output schema, and how it allows us to easily validate the output of the scraper, and ensure that it is in the correct format for the frontend to use, e.g. classifying the menu items into categories like mains, sides, drinks, and ensuring that the prices are in the correct format.

# Section explaining the value of MCP in this instance

Explaining what Model Context Protocol is from Anthropic, and how it has risen to popularity. 

Explanation of the MCP adk, and then FastMCP, which is a Python library that makes it easy to create MCP servers, and define input and output schemas using pydantic models.

Short code section showing in the slides the code needed to make the scraper an MCP server, and how to define the input and output schemas, as a way to explain MCP. 

# Section on deploying the solution

Talk about Azure container apps, and how we can easily deploy our FastAPI backend and MCP server to Azure, and how we can use Azure's managed services to handle scaling and load balancing.

# Concluding section on what did we learn
